items.prompt_md: Python's `heapq` only implements a min-heap. How does the last-stone-weight solution track the running **maximum**?
options: It calls `heapq.nlargest` before every round
It negates every value when pushing and negates again when popping
It keeps a separate variable tracking the max alongside the heap
It re-heapifies the whole list after every round
items.answer: It negates every value when pushing and negates again when popping
items.explanation_md: Storing `-value` makes the largest original value the smallest stored value, so it lands at the min-heap's root. Negate again on the way out to recover the real value.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of peeking at the minimum (`heap[0]`) in a binary heap?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: The heap invariant guarantees the minimum always sits at index 0, so reading it requires no traversal at all.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of `heapq.heappush`?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(log n)
items.explanation_md: Pushing appends the value and then 'sifts' it up at most the height of the heap, which is O(log n).

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of `heapq.heappop`?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(log n)
items.explanation_md: Popping removes the root, moves the last element there, and sifts it down at most the height of the heap, which is O(log n).

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of `heapq.heapify` on an existing list of n elements?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: Building a heap bottom-up from an unordered list takes linear time — cheaper than n individual O(log n) insertions.

items.prompt_md: Why is `heapify` O(n) rather than O(n log n), even though inserting n elements one at a time via `heappush` would cost O(n log n)?
options: It isn't actually O(n) — that's a common misconception
Building bottom-up, most nodes are near the leaves and need very few swaps, so the total work across all nodes sums to O(n)
Python secretly sorts the list first, which is free
heapify only checks the first element and assumes the rest is fine
items.answer: Building bottom-up, most nodes are near the leaves and need very few swaps, so the total work across all nodes sums to O(n)
items.explanation_md: Sifting down from the bottom means the (many) near-leaf nodes barely move, while only the (few) near-root nodes sift far — the sum of that work is linear, not n log n.

items.prompt_md: In Python's `heapq` (a min-heap only), how do you track a running **maximum**?
options: Negate values going in and negate them again coming out
Call `heapq.heappop` twice per round
Keep a separate max variable and ignore the heap
Switch to a different heap library automatically
items.answer: Negate values going in and negate them again coming out
items.explanation_md: There is no built-in max-heap in `heapq` — negation is the standard trick to reuse the min-heap for max-tracking.

items.prompt_md: After popping the two largest stones (`a = -heappop(heap)`, `b = -heappop(heap)`) and computing their difference, what must happen if that difference is still meaningful (non-zero)?
options: Discard it — only the original stones matter
Push its negation back onto the heap so it competes correctly in future rounds
Store it in a hash map instead of the heap
Sort the heap around the new value
items.answer: Push its negation back onto the heap so it competes correctly in future rounds
items.explanation_md: The leftover value re-enters the same pool and must be negated on the way in, just like every other value, to keep the max-heap-via-negation trick consistent.

items.prompt_md: For n elements where each round removes the two largest and pushes back at most one derived value (so the pool shrinks by at least one per round), what is the total time complexity across all rounds?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: There are O(n) rounds, and each round does a constant number of O(log n) heap operations, giving O(n log n) overall.

items.prompt_md: What breaks if you forget to negate values before pushing them, but still expect the heap to track a running maximum?
options: Nothing — heapq automatically detects you want a max
`heap[0]` reports the current minimum, not the maximum, silently giving wrong answers
The program throws a runtime error immediately
It only breaks when there are duplicate values
items.answer: `heap[0]` reports the current minimum, not the maximum, silently giving wrong answers
items.explanation_md: `heapq` has no idea you 'meant' a max-heap — without negation it faithfully tracks the minimum, and the bug is silent rather than an error.

items.prompt_md: Why can a heap answer 'give me the current extremum' without knowing anything about the rest of the pool?
options: Because the extremum sitting at (or near) the root is a structural invariant maintained after every push/pop, not something recomputed by scanning
Because heapq secretly keeps a fully sorted copy of the data
Because it caches the previous answer and reuses it
Because it only works correctly when all elements are distinct
items.answer: Because the extremum sitting at (or near) the root is a structural invariant maintained after every push/pop, not something recomputed by scanning
items.explanation_md: Every push/pop restores the heap property, so the root is always correct by construction — no scan of the rest of the elements is ever needed.

items.prompt_md: What is the key difference between the size-k heap from the top-k lesson and the running-extremum heap in this lesson?
options: There is no difference — they're the same technique
The top-k heap is capped at size k to track a boundary value; the running-extremum heap holds everything and is used purely to repeatedly pop/push
The running-extremum heap only works with integer values
The top-k heap cannot use negation, but the running-extremum heap can
items.answer: The top-k heap is capped at size k to track a boundary value; the running-extremum heap holds everything and is used purely to repeatedly pop/push
items.explanation_md: Same underlying data structure, different job: bounding size to track a boundary value versus using the full pool as a fast pop-and-push engine.

items.prompt_md: Two heaviest stones are repeatedly smashed together, producing a new stone (or nothing) that's fed back into the same pile, until at most one remains.
options: Sort the pile once and simulate with indices
Use a max-heap (negated min-heap): pop the two heaviest each round, push back any leftover
Use a hash map of stone weights
Use two pointers over the sorted pile
items.answer: Use a max-heap (negated min-heap): pop the two heaviest each round, push back any leftover
items.explanation_md: Repeatedly combining the current two largest and feeding a result back into the same pool is the running-extremum heap pattern.

items.prompt_md: Each round, merge the two smallest piles of coins into one pile, and keep doing this until one pile remains; you need the running smallest pair every round.
options: Re-scan the whole list for the two smallest piles every round
Use a min-heap: pop the two smallest, push the merged pile back
Use a stack of pile sizes
Use BFS over the piles
items.answer: Use a min-heap: pop the two smallest, push the merged pile back
items.explanation_md: A round-based 'combine the two smallest, requeue the result' process is exactly what a heap gives you in O(log n) per round.

items.prompt_md: You must repeatedly find and remove the current maximum from a changing collection, sometimes inserting new values back in, over many operations.
options: Track the max in a single variable and rescan the collection whenever it's removed
Use a max-heap so both removal-of-max and insertion cost O(log n)
Use a hash set of all values
Use two pointers over an unsorted collection
items.answer: Use a max-heap so both removal-of-max and insertion cost O(log n)
items.explanation_md: Repeated 'remove the current extremum, maybe insert something new' operations are the running-extremum heap's exact use case.

items.prompt_md: A simulation repeatedly needs 'the current smallest value in the pool,' and the pool is mutated (some values removed, new ones added) every step.
options: Sort the pool from scratch after every mutation
Use a min-heap so peek/pop of the smallest and insertion are both O(log n)
Use a hash map counting how many times each value appears
Use a stack that tracks insertion order
items.answer: Use a min-heap so peek/pop of the smallest and insertion are both O(log n)
items.explanation_md: A constantly mutating pool with a repeated 'current minimum' query is a textbook running-extremum heap.

items.prompt_md: You need to repeatedly combine the two largest weights in a set, feeding the combined weight back in, without ever needing the full sorted order of the set.
options: Maintain the full sorted order at all times with an array
Use a max-heap and only ever look at the top one or two elements
Use a hash map keyed by weight
Use two pointers from each end of a sorted array
items.answer: Use a max-heap and only ever look at the top one or two elements
items.explanation_md: Since only the current top one or two elements ever matter, a heap gives you that without the wasted cost of maintaining a full ordering.

items.prompt_md: A round-based game removes the strongest player, weakens them, and puts them back into the pool, over and over.
options: Rescan for the strongest player each round in O(n)
Use a max-heap: pop the strongest, push back the weakened value
Use a hash map keyed by player id
Use a stack of players in join order
items.answer: Use a max-heap: pop the strongest, push back the weakened value
items.explanation_md: Pop-the-extremum-then-requeue-a-derived-value, repeated over many rounds, is precisely the running-extremum heap pattern.

items.prompt_md: You must process a changing set where the only operation you ever need is 'give me the current minimum, then let me possibly replace it with something else.'
options: Keep the set as an unsorted list and scan for the minimum each time
Use a min-heap: pop gives the minimum in O(log n), push inserts the replacement in O(log n)
Use a hash map of value frequencies
Use two pointers converging from the ends
items.answer: Use a min-heap: pop gives the minimum in O(log n), push inserts the replacement in O(log n)
items.explanation_md: A 'pop the min, push a replacement' loop is the heap's core operation, with no need for the rest of the set to be ordered.

items.prompt_md: Rocks of various hardness are repeatedly crushed two-at-a-time (the two hardest), and the byproduct re-enters the batch, until at most one rock remains.
options: Sort the batch once at the start and never touch it again
Use a max-heap so each round's 'two hardest' pop and the byproduct push are both O(log n)
Use a hash map keyed by hardness value
Use a stack that always crushes the most recently added rock
items.answer: Use a max-heap so each round's 'two hardest' pop and the byproduct push are both O(log n)
items.explanation_md: A static one-time sort can't reflect the byproducts created mid-simulation; only a heap that's re-queried each round handles that.

items.prompt_md: At every step of a long simulation, you need whichever element is currently largest, and after using it you'll often reinsert a modified version.
options: Track a running max variable and manually rescan whenever it changes
Use a max-heap: the root is always the current largest, and reinsertion costs O(log n)
Use a hash map keyed by element id
Use two pointers scanning inward
items.answer: Use a max-heap: the root is always the current largest, and reinsertion costs O(log n)
items.explanation_md: Repeated 'read the current max, then mutate and reinsert' steps are exactly what the running-extremum heap is built for.

items.prompt_md: You need to know whether an array contains any duplicate values at all.
options: Use a max-heap to check the two largest values each round
Use a hash set: insert each value and check if it's already present
Use two pointers from both ends
Use a stack to track values
items.answer: Use a hash set: insert each value and check if it's already present
items.explanation_md: There's no running extremum or repeated combine-and-requeue here — a single hash-set membership check per element solves it directly.

items.prompt_md: Check whether a string of parentheses is balanced and properly nested.
options: Use a min-heap of characters
Use a stack: push opening brackets, pop and match on closing brackets
Use a hash map counting bracket types
Use two pointers from both ends of the string
items.answer: Use a stack: push opening brackets, pop and match on closing brackets
items.explanation_md: Matching nested pairs in last-opened-first-closed order is the stack pattern, not a heap — order of opening matters, not magnitude.

items.prompt_md: Given a binary tree, find its maximum depth.
options: Use a max-heap of node values
Recurse (DFS) down each subtree, taking 1 + the max of the children's depths
Use a hash map keyed by node value
Use two pointers walking the tree's leaves
items.answer: Recurse (DFS) down each subtree, taking 1 + the max of the children's depths
items.explanation_md: Depth is a structural property of the tree computed via recursion, not a running extremum over a flat pool of values — no heap needed.
